title: Graph Neural Networks
description: GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, message passing, spectral vs spatial GNNs, graph pooling, node/edge/graph-level tasks, and scalability techniques.
details: Graph Neural Networks extend deep learning to graph-structured data. This file covers the message-passing framework (aggregate + update), spectral GNNs (graph Laplacian, ChebNet, GCN), spatial GNNs (GraphSAGE, GAT with multi-head attention), graph pooling (DiffPool, TopK, SAGPool), inductive vs transductive learning, scalability techniques (neighbor sampling, cluster-GCN), graph-level tasks (graph classification via readout), edge-level tasks (link prediction), graph generation, common graph benchmarks (Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, OGB), expressiveness limits and the WL test, and position encoding (Laplacian PE, random walk PE). GNNs are increasingly tested in ML interviews (especially for recommendation and drug discovery roles).

sections.title: Message Passing Framework
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Each node aggregates information from its neighbors, then updates its own representation. After k layers, each node's representation contains information from its k-hop neighborhood — akin to a CNN's receptive field growing with depth.

Generic message passing: hv(k) = UPDATE(k)( hv(k-1), AGGREGATE(k)( { hu(k-1) : u ∈ N(v) } ) )

  • AGGREGATE: Permutation-invariant function over neighbors. Options: sum (most expressive), mean, max, attention-weighted sum, LSTM (requires ordering — not permutation-invariant).
  • UPDATE: Combine node's own representation with aggregated neighbor info. Options: concatenate + linear + nonlinear, GRU-style update, residual connection.
  • READOUT: For graph-level tasks: aggregate all node features after final layer. Options: global mean, sum, max, attention, Set Transformer.

Design choices:

  • Self-loops: Add edge from node to itself so AGGREGATE includes own features. Simpler than concatenation, but mixes self and neighbor information.
  • Normalization: Degree normalization prevents node degree from dominating. GCN uses symmetric normalization: Σ 1/√(dvdu) · hu.
  • Over-smoothing: With many layers, all node representations converge to the same value (they aggregate the same neighborhood). Mitigations: skip connections, JK-net, DropEdge, PairNorm, deeper GCN variants.
  • Over-squashing: Information from distant nodes is squashed into fixed-size vectors. Mitigations: virtual nodes, rewiring (DIGL, SDRF), or graph transformers.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import MessagePassing
from torch_geometric.utils import add_self_loops, degree

# === Custom Message Passing Layer ===
class GCNConv(MessagePassing):
    """Simplified GCN convolution."""
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super().__init__(aggr='mean')  # mean aggregation
        self.lin = nn.Linear(in_channels, out_channels)
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        # Add self-loops
        edge_index, _ = add_self_loops(edge_index, num_nodes=x.size(0))
        
        # Linearly transform
        x = self.lin(x)
        
        # Normalize by degree (symmetric normalization)
        row, col = edge_index
        deg = degree(col, x.size(0), dtype=x.dtype)
        deg_inv_sqrt = deg.pow(-0.5)
        norm = deg_inv_sqrt[row] * deg_inv_sqrt[col]
        
        # Message passing
        return self.propagate(edge_index, x=x, norm=norm)
    
    def message(self, x_j, norm):
        # x_j: neighbor features
        return norm.view(-1, 1) * x_j
    
    def update(self, aggr_out):
        # aggr_out: aggregated neighbor messages
        return aggr_out

# === Full GNN for node classification ===
class GNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, hidden_dim, out_dim, num_layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.convs = nn.ModuleList()
        self.convs.append(GCNConv(in_dim, hidden_dim))
        for _ in range(num_layers - 1):
            self.convs.append(GCNConv(hidden_dim, hidden_dim))
        self.final = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, out_dim)
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        for conv in self.convs:
            x = conv(x, edge_index)
            x = F.relu(x)
            x = F.dropout(x, p=0.2, training=self.training)
        return self.final(x)

# === Message types (choose aggregation carefully) ===
# sum: most expressive (can distinguish multisets)
# mean: good for node features + degree info
# max: good for identifying representative neighbors
# 
# The WL (Weisfeiler-Lehman) test limit:
# Sum aggregation matches WL test expressiveness.
# Mean and max are less expressive than sum.
# To match WL: use sum + one-hot degree encoding.

sections.title: GCN, GAT & GraphSAGE
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: GCN averages neighbor features (weighted by degree). GAT learns which neighbors matter via attention. GraphSAGE samples a fixed number of neighbors and handles inductive (unseen node) settings. Each makes different tradeoffs between expressiveness, efficiency, and generalization.

GCN (Graph Convolutional Network):

  • Simplification of ChebNet (first-order approximation of spectral graph convolution).
  • hv = σ( W · Σ αuv hu ) where αuv = 1/√(dudv).
  • Transductive: cannot generalize to unseen graphs without retraining.
  • Fast, simple, good for homophilic graphs (similar nodes connect).

GAT (Graph Attention Network):

  • hv = σ( Σ αvu W hu ) where αvu = softmax( LeakyReLU( aT[W hv || W hu] ) ).
  • Multi-head attention: K independent attention heads, concatenate or average. Stabilizes training.
  • More expressive than GCN (can ignore irrelevant neighbors).
  • Inductive: attention weights depend only on node features, not global structure.

GraphSAGE:

  • Sample fixed-size neighborhood (e.g., 10, 25 neighbors) instead of full neighborhood. Handles high-degree nodes.
  • Aggregators: mean, LSTM (on random permutation), pooling (max or mean of neighbor MLP outputs).
  • hv = σ( W · [hv || AGG({hu : u ∈ N(v)})] ).
  • Designed for inductive learning on large graphs.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === Graph Attention Layer ===
class GATLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim, num_heads=4, dropout=0.2):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.out_dim = out_dim
        
        # Linear projection for each head
        self.W = nn.Linear(in_dim, out_dim * num_heads, bias=False)
        
        # Attention vector for each head
        self.a = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(num_heads, 2 * out_dim))
        nn.init.xavier_uniform_(self.a)
        
        self.leaky_relu = nn.LeakyReLU(0.2)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
    
    def forward(self, x, adj):
        # x: [N, in_dim], adj: [N, N] sparse or dense
        N = x.size(0)
        
        # Linear projection + reshape for multi-head
        x = self.W(x)  # [N, H*D]
        x = x.view(N, self.num_heads, self.out_dim)  # [N, H, D]
        
        # Compute attention scores for all pairs
        # a^T [W h_i || W h_j]
        x_i = x.unsqueeze(2).expand(-1, -1, N, -1)  # [N, H, N, D]
        x_j = x.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, N, -1, -1)  # [N, N, H, D]
        
        # Concatenate
        concat = torch.cat([x_i, x_j], dim=-1)  # [N, H, N, 2D]
        
        # Compute attention (batch over heads)
        e = torch.einsum('nhid,hid->nh', concat, self.a)  # [N, H, N]
        e = self.leaky_relu(e)
        
        # Mask by adjacency
        e = e.masked_fill(adj == 0, float('-inf'))
        
        # Softmax over neighbors
        alpha = F.softmax(e, dim=-1)  # [N, H, N]
        alpha = self.dropout(alpha)
        
        # Weighted sum of neighbor features
        h_prime = torch.einsum('nhj,njd->nhd', alpha, x)  # [N, H, D]
        
        # Concatenate heads (or average for final layer)
        h_prime = h_prime.reshape(N, -1)  # [N, H*D]
        return h_prime

# === GraphSAGE ===
class SAGELayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim, aggr='mean'):
        super().__init__()
        self.aggr = aggr
        self.W = nn.Linear(in_dim * 2, out_dim)
        
        if aggr == 'pool':
            self.pool_mlp = nn.Sequential(
                nn.Linear(in_dim, out_dim),
                nn.ReLU()
            )
    
    def forward(self, x, neighbor_feats):
        """
        x: [N, D] node features
        neighbor_feats: [N, S, D] sampled neighbor features
        """
        if self.aggr == 'mean':
            agg = neighbor_feats.mean(dim=1)  # [N, D]
        elif self.aggr == 'max':
            agg = neighbor_feats.max(dim=1)[0]
        elif self.aggr == 'pool':
            agg = self.pool_mlp(neighbor_feats).max(dim=1)[0]
        
        # Concatenate self + aggregated neighbor
        combined = torch.cat([x, agg], dim=1)  # [N, 2D]
        return F.relu(self.W(combined))

# === Simplified GCN layer ===
def gcn_layer(A_hat, X, W):
    """
    A_hat: normalized adjacency with self-loops
    X: node features
    W: weight matrix
    """
    return F.relu(A_hat @ X @ W)

sections.title: Scalability & Pooling
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Full-batch GNN training doesn't scale beyond ~100K nodes. Neighbor sampling, cluster-GCN, and graph partitioning enable mini-batch training. Pooling reduces graph size for hierarchical representations and graph classification.

Scalability techniques:

  • Neighbor sampling (GraphSAGE): Sample fixed number of neighbors per node per layer. “SAGE-style sampling.” Limits receptive field to O(SL) where S = samples per layer, L = layers.
  • Cluster-GCN: Partition graph into clusters via METIS or LPA. Train on one cluster (and its intra-cluster edges) per mini-batch. Avoids neighbor explosion. Has a regularization effect.
  • GraphSAINT: Sample subgraphs (not node neighborhoods) and train on full subgraph. Lower variance than node-wise sampling.
  • AS-GCN (Adaptive Sampling): Learn importance sampling distribution for neighbors. Reduces variance further.

Pooling methods:

  • Global pooling: Mean, sum, max of all node features. Simple, loses graph structure.
  • DiffPool: Learn a soft assignment matrix S = softmax(GNN(A, X)). Sik = probability node i belongs to cluster k. Coarsen graph: X' = STX, A' = STAS. End-to-end differentiable.
  • TopK Pooling: Score nodes by projection vector p. Keep top K nodes by score. Hard assignment, not differentiable (use straight-through or gumbel-softmax).
  • SAGPool: Self-attention graph pooling. Use GNN to compute attention scores, keep top K nodes. Combines topology and features for scoring.
  • Virtual nodes: Add a “supernode” connected to all nodes. Its representation serves as graph-level readout. Simple, effective.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === Neighbor sampling (mini-batch GNN) ===
def sample_neighbors(edge_index, nodes, num_samples=10):
    """
    Sample num_samples neighbors for each node.
    edge_index: [2, E]
    nodes: [B] node indices in batch
    Returns: [B, S] sampled neighbor indices
    """
    # Build adjacency
    N = edge_index.max().item() + 1
    adj = [[] for _ in range(N)]
    for u, v in edge_index.T:
        adj[u].append(v)
        adj[v].append(u)  # undirected
    
    sampled = []
    for node in nodes:
        neighbors = adj[node.item()]
        if len(neighbors) >= num_samples:
            idx = torch.randperm(len(neighbors))[:num_samples]
            sampled.append([neighbors[i] for i in idx])
        else:
            # Pad with random neighbors
            sampled.append(neighbors + 
                list(torch.randint(0, N, (num_samples - len(neighbors),))))
    return torch.tensor(sampled)

# === DiffPool-style soft assignment ===
class DiffPool(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim, num_clusters):
        super().__init__()
        # GNN for node features
        self.pool_gnn = nn.Linear(in_dim, out_dim)
        # GNN for assignment
        self.assign_gnn = nn.Linear(in_dim, num_clusters)
    
    def forward(self, X, adj):
        # Node features
        Z = F.relu(self.pool_gnn(X))  # [N, D]
        # Cluster assignment
        S = F.softmax(self.assign_gnn(X), dim=1)  # [N, C]
        
        # New node features
        X_new = S.T @ Z  # [C, D] (weighted sum per cluster)
        # New adjacency (coarsened)
        A_new = S.T @ adj @ S  # [C, C]
        
        return X_new, A_new, S

# === TopK pooling ===
class TopKPooling(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, ratio=0.5):
        super().__init__()
        self.score_proj = nn.Linear(in_dim, 1)
        self.ratio = ratio
    
    def forward(self, X, adj, edge_index):
        scores = self.score_proj(X).squeeze()  # [N]
        k = max(1, int(X.size(0) * self.ratio))
        
        # Keep top K
        values, indices = torch.topk(scores, k)
        mask = torch.zeros(X.size(0), dtype=torch.bool)
        mask[indices] = True
        
        # Filter nodes
        X_pooled = X[indices] * values.view(-1, 1)  # gate by score
        
        # Filter edges (both endpoints must be in mask)
        edge_mask = mask[edge_index[0]] & mask[edge_index[1]]
        edge_index_pooled = edge_index[:, edge_mask]
        
        # Remap node indices to 0..k-1
        node_mapping = {old.item(): new for new, old in enumerate(indices)}
        for i in range(2):
            for j in range(edge_index_pooled.size(1)):
                edge_index_pooled[i, j] = node_mapping[edge_index_pooled[i, j].item()]
        
        return X_pooled, edge_index_pooled

# === Readout: Virtual Node ===
class VirtualNodeGNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, hidden_dim, out_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.virtual_node = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(1, hidden_dim))
        self.gnn = GCNConv(in_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.readout = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, out_dim)
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        # Add virtual node to each graph in batch
        # Concatenate virtual node to node features
        # Connect virtual node to all nodes
        # (implementation depends on PyG batch format)
        h = self.gnn(x, edge_index)
        # Graph readout: virtual node representation
        # (or global mean pooling)
        return self.readout(h[:len(batch)])

sections.title: Graph Tasks & Expressiveness
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Not all GNNs are equally powerful. The WL (Weisfeiler-Lehman) test defines the upper bound of message-passing GNN expressiveness. Tasks go beyond node classification to edges and entire graphs — each requires different architectural choices.

Graph tasks:

  • Node classification: Predict label for each node (e.g., paper topic in citation network). Standard semi-supervised learning (few labeled nodes). Best benchmark: ogbn-arxiv.
  • Link prediction: Predict if edge exists between two nodes. Decoders: dot product, DistMult, or MLP on node embeddings. Negative sampling (sample non-edges) required. Use in recommendation (user-item graph).
  • Graph classification: Predict label for entire graph (e.g., molecular property prediction). Use global pooling after message passing. Best benchmark: ogbg-molhiv, ogbg-ppa.
  • Edge classification: Predict type/relation of an edge. Common in knowledge graphs.** WL test expressiveness:**
    • GCN + mean = GIN-0 (mean aggregates) ≤ WL.
    • GCN + sum = GIN (sum aggregates) = WL. Can distinguish any two graphs WL can.
    • No message-passing GNN can distinguish regular graphs (all nodes have same degree and features) — WL also fails.
    • Higher-order GNNs (k-GNNs, PPGNs, equivariant) can go beyond WL but are expensive.

Position encoding: Standard MP-GNNs are permutation-equivariant — node ordering doesn't matter. But tasks may depend on positional information (distances, angles). Solutions: Laplacian eigenvectors, random walk structural encoding, distance-to-landmark encoding.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, accuracy_score
import torch_geometric
from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv, global_mean_pool, global_add_pool

# === Link Prediction ===
class LinkPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, hidden_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.gnn = GCNConv(in_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim * 2, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1),
            nn.Sigmoid()
        )
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index, pos_edges, neg_edges):
        # Node embeddings
        z = self.gnn(x, edge_index)
        z = F.relu(z)
        
        # Positive edges
        pos_out = self.decoder(
            torch.cat([z[pos_edges[0]], z[pos_edges[1]]], dim=1)
        )
        # Negative edges
        neg_out = self.decoder(
            torch.cat([z[neg_edges[0]], z[neg_edges[1]]], dim=1)
        )
        return pos_out, neg_out
    
    def loss(self, pos_out, neg_out):
        pos_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy(pos_out, torch.ones_like(pos_out))
        neg_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy(neg_out, torch.zeros_like(neg_out))
        return pos_loss + neg_loss

# === Graph Classification ===
class GraphClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, node_dim, hidden_dim, num_classes):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(node_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.conv3 = GCNConv(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, num_classes)
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        # Message passing
        x = self.conv1(x, edge_index).relu()
        x = self.conv2(x, edge_index).relu()
        x = self.conv3(x, edge_index).relu()
        
        # Global pooling (per graph via batch vector)
        x = global_mean_pool(x, batch)  # [num_graphs, hidden_dim]
        
        return self.classifier(x)

# === Expressiveness: WL test ===
# GIN (Graph Isomorphism Network) = sum aggregation + MLP update
# Matches WL test exactly

class GINConv(nn.Module):
    """1-WL equivalent convolution."""
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_dim, out_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(out_dim, out_dim),
        )
        self.eps = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(0.0))
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        # Sum aggregation (most expressive)
        row, col = edge_index
        neighbor_sum = torch.zeros_like(x)
        neighbor_sum.index_add_(0, col, x[row])  # sum neighbors
        return self.mlp((1 + self.eps) * x + neighbor_sum)

# === Laplacian Position Encoding ===
def laplacian_pe(edge_index, num_nodes, pe_dim=8):
    """Precompute Laplacian eigenvectors as positional encoding."""
    # Compute normalized Laplacian L = I - D^(-1/2) A D^(-1/2)
    from torch_geometric.utils import get_laplacian
    from scipy.sparse.linalg import eigsh
    import numpy as np
    
    edge_index, edge_weight = get_laplacian(edge_index, num_nodes=num_nodes)
    L = torch.sparse_coo_tensor(edge_index, edge_weight, 
                                 (num_nodes, num_nodes))
    L = L.to_dense().numpy()
    
    # Compute smallest eigenvectors (skip trivial eigenvector)
    eigvals, eigvecs = eigsh(L, k=pe_dim + 1, which='SM')
    return eigvecs[:, 1:pe_dim + 1].astype(np.float32)  # [N, pe_dim]
    # Concatenate to node features as positional encoding
